# 

| Corduroy Platform: Technical Design Document                 | Jun 18, 2026 |
| :---- | ----: |

# Launch 1 — The Vault (Core Framework) and the Planner

Working codename: The Corduroy Business Advisory Platform \[BAP\]

| Field | Value |
| :---- | :---- |
| Version | 0.3 — Draft for review |
| Date | June 18, 2026 |
| Author | Michael |
| Status | Working draft. v0.3 details the Vault data plane: two-Lambda retrieval, append-only ingestion, the Postgres catalog, failure/recovery, and a forward-looking widget note. One position remains to confirm — see callout below. |
| Scope | Launch 1 only. KPI Dashboard and Daily Coach \+ HITL are Launch 2 (summarized in §11). |

## Executive Summary

Corduroy Launch 1 establishes the secure foundation for the company's AI-assisted business advisory platform. The release consists of two core capabilities: the Vault, a secure client data repository, and the Planner, an AI-powered planning system that converts client information and audit documents into actionable business plans reviewed by human advisors.

The design prioritizes security, client isolation, and human oversight. Each client's data is stored separately, with strict controls ensuring that neither other clients nor unauthorized staff can access it. Sensitive business documents remain in a dedicated secure storage environment, while AI processing occurs through a carefully controlled workflow designed to minimize data exposure. Every access, upload, and change is logged for auditability.

From a user perspective, clients can upload business documents, view stored materials, and receive and interact with customized action plans. Advisors can review, refine, and approve all AI-generated plans and recommendations before they reach clients, ensuring the system delivers practical guidance rather than generic outputs.

The architecture is intentionally designed to support future expansion. While Launch 1 focuses on secure data management and planning, the platform is structured to accommodate upcoming capabilities such as KPI dashboards, automated business metrics, daily coaching workflows, and integrations with systems like QuickBooks.

Overall, Launch 1 creates the trusted data and planning foundation upon which the broader Corduroy advisory platform will be built.

## 1\. Purpose & scope

This document defines the engineering design for Launch 1 of the Corduroy system: the Vault (the secure per-client data foundation) and the 90-Day Planner (AI-generated execution plans with human review). It is the build-team reference for sprint planning, infrastructure provisioning, and security review.

Launch 1 maps to Phases 1–2 of the Build Plan. The **KPI Dashboard** and **Daily Coaching \+ HITL** layers are explicitly out of scope here and arrive in Launch 2; they are summarized in §11 so the Launch 1 architecture leaves room for them. Third-party connectors (QuickBooks et al.) and desktop agentic retrieval remain Phase 2 and are not designed here.

| Architecture settled for Launch 1 The major structural decisions are now made: one Supabase project with role-based access, one codebase with separate subdomains, and a two-Lambda retrieval service in AWS. What remains open (§10) is vendor confirmation rather than architecture — Supabase's SAML SSO tier and SOC 2 report, and the Anthropic zero-data-retention terms. |
| :---- |

## 2\. System overview & architecture

The \[BAP\] is organized into five planes. The guiding principle is that the sensitive data plane lives entirely in AWS and is reachable only through a single brokered service; everything outside AWS holds business logic and identity, never raw client files or storage credentials.

### **Architecture planes**

![][image1]

| Plane | Components | Host |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Presentation** | Client App \+ Admin Console (one codebase, separate subdomains) | Vercel (one project) |
| **Identity** | Single auth pool; role-based; staff via SSO \+ MFA (email-domain routing) | Supabase (one project) |
| **Application** | Orchestration API: business logic, plan generation, LLM calls, audit writes | Railway Pro |
| **Relational data** | Clients, users, plans, milestones, tasks, audit metadata (Postgres \+ RLS) | Supabase Postgres |
| **Data plane (Vault)** | Per-client S3 buckets (SSE-KMS, append-only dated objects) | AWS (VPC) |
| **Retrieval Service** | Small, simple code that handles interactions with the files in the Vault | AWS (Lambda) |
| **LLM** | Plan/context generation via Anthropic API, zero-data-retention tier | Anthropic API (ZDR) |

### 

| Why the Retrieval Service lives in AWS (not Railway) VPC endpoints only protect S3 traffic if the calling compute is inside the VPC. A retrieval layer on Railway would reach S3 over the public internet, so the VPC-endpoint requirement would be cosmetic. Keeping the one credential-holding component inside AWS means IAM roles, per-client KMS keys, VPC endpoints, and the ZDR LLM hand-off all compose natively, and S3 credentials never leave AWS. The “LLMs and agents never hold S3 credentials” principle becomes structural, not just policy. Railway still hosts everything else (the orchestration API). A Railway outage degrades the app tier but cannot expose client storage. |  |  |
| :---- | ----- | ----- |

### **Key request flows for file exchanges**

#### **A) Client uploads a file**

1. Client authenticates to the Client App via SSO \+ mandatory MFA.  
2. Client App requests an upload slot from the Orchestration API, authenticated with the client JSON Web Token (JWT).  
3. Orchestration API verifies the JWT, confirms the user's client\_id, and asks the **Access Broker** (on AWS/Lambda) to mint a short-lived pre-signed S3 PUT URL for a server-built key inside that client's raw/ prefix.  
4. Client’s browser uploads directly to S3 via the pre-signed URL; neither the API nor the broker proxies file bytes.  
5. S3’s ObjectCreated action fires the **Content Processor** (on AWS/Lambda), which completes the audit row,  updates the catalog (see below), extracts contents, generates context files.  
*  (`vault_objects`) is the directory — one row per object describing what exists in S3: key, prefix, type, source, size, created\_at. Its job is to answer "what does this client have, and which is the latest." It's a current-state index.  
* The **audit log** (`audit_events`) is the history of actions — one append-only row per access or write event: who, which client, which object, when, why. Its job is the access trail for the security story.

  #### **B) Staff accesses a client file**

1. Advisor authenticates to the Admin Console via SSO \+ mandatory MFA.  
2. Admin Console requests a specific object; **Orchestration API** verifies the staff token and the advisor's assignment to that client.  
3. **Access Broker** mints a pre-signed GET URL that expires in minutes and writes an audit row including the stated reason; the browser pulls the bytes directly from S3.

   #### **C) Planner generates a 90-day plan**

1. Advisor triggers generation for a client. API invokes the content processor, which reads the relevant context (Audit document \+ Vault context files) from S3.  
2. The content processor calls the Anthropic API on a zero-data-retention tier from inside AWS, so raw client content never reaches Railway.  
3. The structured plan JSON is written to the client's derived/ prefix by the content processor, and structured rows are upserted into Postgres for querying.  
4. The plan enters the advisor review queue; nothing reaches the client until an advisor approves it.

## 3\. Technology stack & rationale

| Layer | Choice | Role | Notes / watch items |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Frontend | Next.js on Vercel Pro | One codebase; Admin Console on its own subdomain (admin.corduroy.com) with its own middleware | Admin logic stays server-side; option to IP-allowlist or SSO-gate the admin subdomain later without a rebuild. |
| App / API | Railway Pro | Orchestration API: business logic, plan generation, LLM orchestration, audit writes | Reliability is the watch item; sensitive data plane stays in AWS so blast radius is limited. Render / Fly / Fargate are fallbacks. |
| Identity | Supabase Auth (one project) | Single auth pool; staff vs client by role. Staff sign in via SSO \+ MFA, routed by email domain | Confirm Pro tier includes SAML SSO. Split into separate projects later only if an enterprise security review demands it. |
| Relational DB | Supabase Postgres \+ RLS | Plans, milestones, tasks, users, audit metadata | Row-level security scopes every query to a client\_id. |
| Storage (Vault) | AWS S3 \+ KMS | Per-client bucket; SSE-KMS, append-only dated objects, VPC  (virtual private cloud) endpoints | Per-client buckets are clean at v1; plan prefix/account sharding past \~1,000 clients (bucket limit). |
| Retrieval Service | Two AWS Lambdas (access broker \+ content processor) | AccessBroker mints URLs and audits on the hot path; ContentDispatcher extracts and routes data where it needs to go | Both scoped to one client per call, no delete. ContentDispatcher can move to App Runner/Fargate if extraction outgrows Lambda limits. |
| LLM | Anthropic API (Sonnet 4.6) | Plan and context generation, zero-data-retention tier | Confirm ZDR tier terms before client commitments cite them. |

## 4\. Identity & access

Client-data isolation is enforced by three things working together: API authorization, Postgres row-level security scoped to client\_id, and the Retrieval Service as the sole holder of S3 credentials. None of these depend on separate auth pools, so client and staff share one Supabase project, distinguished by role. Staff are additionally required to authenticate via SSO with enforced MFA.

### **4.1 Client authentication**

* Email/password (optionally magic link) in the shared Supabase project, with MFA available.  
* JWT carries the user's client\_id; Postgres RLS enforces that a client user can only read/write rows for their own client.  
* No client user can see another client's data, plan, or files — verified by security review per the Vault definition of done.

### **4.2 Staff authentication**

* Same Supabase project, with staff signing in via SSO and mandatory MFA. Supabase routes SSO by email domain, so staff (corduroy.ai SAML) and clients (password) coexist in one pool. An approval workflow gates new staff access.  
* Roles: principal, apprentice/advisor, admin. Assignments map advisors to specific clients; an advisor can act only on assigned clients.  
* No principal or advisor has cross-client access or delete access. Destructive operations are not exposed in the Admin Console at all (see §9).

### **4.3 Authorization boundary**

Client-surface and admin-surface endpoints are distinct route groups with distinct middleware, served on separate subdomains from one codebase. The API checks the role claim on every request: admin endpoints require a staff role and reject client tokens outright, and every request — client or staff — is scoped to a client\_id and re-checked against Postgres RLS. The isolation guarantee lives in this authorization layer plus RLS plus the Retrieval Service, not in how many auth projects exist.

## 5\. The Vault — detailed design

### **5.1 Storage layout**

* **One S3 bucket per client**, with a per-client KMS key (SSE-KMS). Strongest isolation boundary at v1 scale.

* Prefix convention within each bucket: **raw/** (source pulls and uploads), **derived/** (plan JSON, extracted artifacts), **context/** (summaries, indices, markdown for AI consumption).

* **Append-only by convention.** Objects are written once, never overwritten in place. A new data pull is a new, date-stamped object (e.g. *raw/quickbooks/2026-06-18.csv*), not an overwrite of last week's. The same applies to the analysis layer: a regenerated summary or plan is written as a new dated object under *derived/* rather than replacing the prior one.

* This preserves full history as named, listable, dated objects — which is more useful for lookback analysis than hidden storage-level versions would be — without any special storage features. The only thing to enforce is a careful write path that never targets an existing key.

* VPC endpoints for S3 and KMS so Retrieval Service traffic stays on the AWS network (only meaningful because the Retrieval Service runs in-VPC).

| Considered and discarded for v1: Object Lock and versioning S3 Object Lock (immutable WORM storage) and bucket versioning were both evaluated and intentionally left out. They defend against accidental or malicious deletion and in-place mutation — durability and tamper problems. Corduroy's real exposure is confidentiality, not durability: this is business data that also lives in the client's own source systems, S3 already provides extremely high durability, and the append-only convention above preserves history without them. Object Lock would also have created tension with any data-return/deletion commitment to clients. Both are cheap to enable later. If a future client or regulatory requirement calls for provable immutability (most likely on the audit trail), versioning and/or Object Lock can be turned on for specific buckets or prefixes without redesigning anything here. |
| :---- |

### **5.2 IAM isolation**

Each client bucket's policy grants access only to the Retrieval Service's execution role, scoped per request to a single client by prefix. There is no human or LLM IAM principal with direct bucket access, and no role spans more than one client's data within a single operation.

### **5.3 The Retrieval Service — two Lambdas**

All Vault I/O flows through AWS Lambda functions that run inside the VPC under IAM execution roles — so KMS access and VPC-endpoint routing are native, and there are no stored credentials anywhere. The service is split into two single-purpose functions, each scoped to one client per invocation, with no delete capability.

#### **Access broker (featherweight, hot path)**

On the path for every human file view and upload. Its entire job is validate, sign, log: confirm the requested key sits inside the caller's client bucket/prefix, mint a short-lived pre-signed URL (GET for reads, PUT for uploads), write an audit row, and return the URL. It never touches file bytes, so each call is milliseconds.

#### **Content processor (heavier, event-driven)**

Longer timeout and more memory. Triggered by an S3 ObjectCreated event when a raw file lands. It reads the object, extracts and normalizes its contents, calls the Anthropic API on a zero-data-retention tier, writes any derived artifacts back to the client's derived/ prefix directly under its own execution role, projects structured output into Postgres, and audits. This is also the Planner's generation worker.

| Why URLs, not bytes A synchronous Lambda response is capped at 6 MB, so proxying real files (a 20 MB export, a fat PDF) through it would fail. Minting a pre-signed URL sidesteps this entirely: the browser transfers bytes directly to/from S3, and the broker's hot-path job stays validate-sign-log. |
| :---- |
| **Execution-role model & least privilege** The Lambdas hold no stored secrets. AWS assumes the execution role at invocation and injects temporary, auto-rotated credentials — nothing in config can rot, leak, or be forgotten. Railway's AWS credentials can do exactly one thing: invoke the Lambda. The backend cannot reach S3 directly even if fully compromised; only the Lambda can, and only in the narrow way its code allows. That is the actual security win, and the reason to keep the functions tiny. |
| **Zero-data-retention LLM path** LLMs never touch S3 and never hold storage credentials. The content processor reads the file, extracts contents, and passes them as context via a zero-data-retention API tier, so the model retains nothing after responding. The Anthropic call runs from inside the content-processor Lambda, so raw client document content never touches Railway — it lives only in S3 and transits only the Lambda→Anthropic ZDR hop. Railway only ever sees structured output (plan metadata, rows), never file contents. Any artifact the model produces is written back to derived/ by the content processor, never by the model directly. |
| **What the Lambdas do and do not enforce** They enforce structural isolation: one client's bucket per call, no cross-client key access, no delete. They are the last gate against storage-layer leakage and least-privilege violations. They do NOT re-decide whether this user may see this client — that authorization lives in the backend plus RLS (§4.3). The Lambda is not a second copy of the auth logic; assuming it is would be a mistake. |

### **5.4 Ingestion & context generation**

Upload flow (mirror of the download flow):

1. Client App tells the backend it wants to upload, with metadata (filename, type, size, source). Backend authenticates and authorizes the user for that client.  
2. Backend invokes the access broker. The broker builds the destination key server-side from client\_id \+ source \+ a server-stamped timestamp \+ a short random suffix (e.g. raw/quickbooks/2026-06-18T14-03-09Z-a1b2.csv), confirms it is inside the client's raw/ prefix, mints a pre-signed PUT URL for that one key, writes a pending audit row, and returns the URL.  
3. Browser PUTs the bytes straight to S3. Neither the broker nor Railway touches the file.  
4. S3 ObjectCreated on raw/ fires the content processor, which flips the audit row from pending to complete and runs extraction \+ context-file generation. The upload completing is itself the trigger — no polling, no orchestration in the backend.

| The broker owns the key — this is how append-only is enforced The browser never proposes the object key; the broker builds it. Because the key is unique by construction, a PUT cannot clobber a sibling file and cannot hit an existing object. Append-only is enforced structurally by the naming convention, doing the job Object Lock and versioning would have (§5.1). |
| :---- |

* **Type validation in two spots:** the backend rejects obviously unsupported files up front (declared content type against the Launch 1 set — PDF, CSV, XLSX, DOCX), and the content processor sniffs the actual bytes after upload rather than trusting the browser's declared type.

* **Derivative writes do not use pre-signed URLs.** Derived files (plan JSON, summaries, markdown) are generated by the content processor and written directly to derived/ under its execution role — an internal AWS-to-S3 write. The PUT-URL path is for browser uploads only; a staff hand-upload of a document would use that same path uniformly, but that is the exception.

* **Context generation on ingest**: the content processor produces summaries, indices, and markdown tuned for AI consumption. Chunking strategy is a Phase 1 experiment — tune window size against real client files.

* **Advisor-led manual ingestion-assist workflow for white-glove onboarding**; automated connectors and desktop agents are deferred to Phase 2\.

* **Deliberately skipped for v1:** multipart uploads (a single PUT handles up to 5 GB and these files are spreadsheets and exports, nowhere close) and S3 POST-policy uploads (which would let a signed URL enforce a hard max-size condition; plain pre-signed PUT is simpler and easy to revisit if server-enforced size caps are ever wanted).

### **5.5 The catalog & directory model**

**Postgres is the directory; the Lambda is the doorway.** Directory questions — what does this client have, which plan is the latest, all context files from QuickBooks in the last 90 days — are answered from a catalog table in Postgres, never from an S3 LIST on the hot path. Discovery (a catalog query) and access (a signed URL via the broker) stay separate: the backend queries the catalog to find which key it wants, then asks the broker for a URL for that one object.

* **Catalog row:** client\_id, key, prefix, type, source, size, created\_at. “Latest” becomes an indexed metadata query rather than parsing timestamps out of filenames.

  * **Why not S3 LIST:** it is paginated at 1,000 keys, cannot express “latest plan” or “only markdown” without listing and filtering client-side, and gets clumsy exactly as a client accumulates the history the lookback story depends on.

* **Self-healing index:** the same ObjectCreated event that completes an upload also writes/reconciles the catalog row, so anything landing in a bucket gets indexed automatically.

* **Reconciliation / true-up**: a housekeeping function (scheduled and on-demand) LISTs each client bucket, compares against the catalog, adds missing rows, and flags orphans. It reconciles, never rebuilds — add what is missing, mark what is absent, never wipe-and-regenerate — so a transient LIST hiccup cannot destroy good catalog data. The broker holds a scoped ListBucket permission reserved for this and for break-glass directory browsing, not the hot path.

| Serving rule: data renders from Postgres, documents fetch through the Lambda S3 through the Lambda is for documents — a human wants the actual PDF, plan doc, or raw export. Discrete, occasional, fine to route through a signed URL. Postgres is for data — a screen needs to render numbers. Constant, latency-sensitive, never touches a file. The dashboard renders entirely from Postgres; the Lambda never sits between a chart and its data. With this line held, the catalog lets the application treat the Vault like a fast local filesystem for everything except the actual byte-fetch. |
| :---- |

### **5.6 Audit log**

Every access and update event records who, which client, which object, when, and why. Audit rows live in Postgres as an append-only log (write-only from the API/Lambdas, with no delete path exposed) and are mirrored to an audit/ prefix in the client bucket. Append-only writes and the absence of a delete path are sufficient tamper-evidence for v1; if provable immutability is ever required, Object Lock can be enabled on this prefix alone (see §5.1).

### **5.7 Failure modes & recovery**

The Lambda path failing degrades the application; it does not endanger the data. Data safety rests on S3 durability and the KMS key surviving — neither depends on the Lambda existing. The Lambda restricts Railway's path to storage, not the account owner's. If the Lambda vanished entirely, the files sit untouched and the account owner can reach them via the console, CLI, or a fresh function in minutes. You are never isolated from your own data; the worst a Lambda failure does is interrupt the live app.

* **No stored keys to lose:** the execution role is an identity AWS assumes at invocation with temporary, auto-rotated credentials, so the “we lost the keys and the files are sealed” scenario is not reachable.

* **Most likely failure — self-inflicted misconfiguration:** a fat-fingered bucket ARN in the role policy or a bad role binding in a deploy. Mitigation: define the role, its policy, and KMS config as infrastructure-as-code (Terraform/CDK), require review to change, and never hand-edit in the console — so a bad change is a revertible diff.

* **The one genuinely dangerous version of “isolated from ourselves” is KMS:** a disabled or deleted per-client key leaves files intact but undecryptable. Mitigation: treat KMS keys as never-delete and tightly hold disable/delete permissions. AWS enforces a 7–30 day waiting period (with alarms) before a key can actually be deleted, which closes this door.

* **Regional disruption:** can make S3 or Lambda unreachable for a window; it resolves on its own and the data is intact and AZ-replicated throughout.

* **Observability & recovery:** a canary health check that mints and uses a pre-signed URL against a canary object end-to-end (pages in minutes, not via a confused client), plus a documented break-glass path — the account owner reaching the data directly without the Lambda, written down before it is needed.

| Keep the response proportionate This protects uptime, not the files. The data's safety does not depend on the Lambda, so the proportionate response is a health check, a break-glass runbook, and the KMS never-delete rule — not architectural redundancy. Be clear which problem is being solved so the wrong one does not get gold-plated. |
| :---- |

| Vault — definition of done (from Build Plan) A real client's data is fully ingested and structured in their bucket. An LLM prompt can retrieve relevant context through the Retrieval Service in under 5 seconds. No cross-client access is possible — verified by security review. An advisor can monitor data freshness and flag gaps from a simple admin view. |
| :---- |

## 

### **5.8 Views — the Vault**

The Vault's user-facing surface is the Vault: a two-pane screen for assembling and monitoring a client's data sources. The left pane adds sources — drag-and-drop upload (PDF, XLSX, DOCX, CSV) with a source label, category, and date-of-data, or a one-click connect to a system (QuickBooks, Pipedrive, Quotient, AnswerConnect; others as they are added). The right pane is the Data Repository: the catalog (§5.5) rendered for humans as a per-client list grouped by category (Financial; Pipeline & Sales; Operations & Context), each source showing a type badge (QB live, XLSX, PDF, API, Web scrape), its last-updated date, and an obtained / awaiting state, topped by a completeness meter (e.g. “8 of 11 sources obtained — 68% complete”).

* **USER STORY** As a client, I can upload a file or connect a system and immediately see it in my repository with its type and freshness, so I know what Corduroy holds.

* **USER STORY** As an advisor, I can see at a glance which sources are obtained versus still awaiting, so I can chase the gaps during white-glove onboarding.

* **USER STORY** As either, the repository reads from the catalog in Postgres — not a live S3 listing — so it loads instantly and reflects accurate “as of” freshness.

**Implementation**: uploads use the AccessBroker pre-signed PUT flow (§5.4); the repository list and completeness meter come from the catalog, kept current by the ObjectCreated event and the reconciliation job (§5.5).

### **![][image2]**

#### 

## 6\. The 90-Day Planner — detailed design

### **6.1 Pipeline**

1. Inputs: the client's Audit document plus Vault context files.

2. Prompt architecture produces a structured 90-day plan (chain-of-thought internally, structured JSON output).

3. Plan JSON is stored in the Vault (derived/) and projected into Postgres rows for querying and the client view.

4. Plan enters the advisor review queue; advisor edits before anything reaches the client.

5. Client-facing view: read the plan, mark items complete, comment. Client cannot edit plan structure in Launch 1\.

### **6.2 Plan schema**

| Field | Type | Description |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| week | int (1–13) | Calendar week within the 90-day window. |
| milestone | string | Concrete, client-specific milestone (not a template line). |
| assignee | enum | Client owner, Corduroy, or both (e.g. a meeting). |
| due\_date | date | Target date; week-level granularity acceptable at first pass. |
| priority | enum | High / medium / low for sequencing. |
| dependencies | array | Other milestone IDs this one depends on. |
| status | enum | Not started / in progress / done / stuck (client-set). |
| comments | array | Threaded notes from client and advisor. |

### **6.3 Advisor review & quality signal**

* Advisor review queue shows generated plans pending approval, with edit tools.

* **Human edit rate is tracked as the quality signal**. Target: advisors make fewer than 20% substantive edits on average. Categorize what advisors change most — it shows where the prompt architecture needs work.

| Planner — definition of done (from Build Plan) A plan generated from real Vault data is reviewed by an advisor and called actionable — not generic. Advisor makes fewer than 20% substantive edits on average. A client, shown their plan cold, can describe their top 3 next actions without help. |
| :---- |

### 

## 6.4 Views — the plan, client and consultant

**Implementation:** both surfaces render from the Postgres plan projection (§5.5 serving rule); the underlying plan JSON in derived/ is the provenance record, not what the screens read.

### **6.4 Views**

#### **Planner View.**

Displays a list of tasks and progress derived from the Audit document, allowing for interactivity by the client: checkoffs, checkins, notes and commentary, progress metrics. The client 90-Day Plan view presents the week-by-week plan: a left rail of weeks with per-week completion, the current week's theme and status header (e.g. “Week 3 — Commercial push & follow-up · On track · 8 tasks remaining”), day tabs, milestone-progress bars, and tasks grouped into Due today, In progress, and Completed — each task carrying an owner, a category tag (Sales, Pipeline, Admin), and a line of context. In Launch 1 the client can read, mark complete, and comment; they cannot restructure the plan.

* As a client, shown my plan cold, I can identify my top three next actions and what is due today without help (the Planner definition of done).

![][image3]

#### 

#### **Staff View: Planner.**

Allows staffers to see high-level views of relevant data (tasks, metrics, files, notes, including staff-only) for any selected client (sorted by Needs attention vs. On track. For the selected client a milestone list in consultant form with an Edit plan control, plus consultant observations and a weekly action list. The KPI cards and the draft-coach-message panel that appear in the mockups belong to Launch 2 (the Dashboard and Coach) and are shown there for context, not built in Launch 1\. Also allows for upload/download of original and derived files to a client’s Vault). 

* **USER STORY**: As an advisor, I can review and edit a generated plan before the client ever sees it (the review queue), and see across my portfolio which clients need attention.

* **USER STORY:** As an advisor, I can open any client's plan in consultant form and edit milestones, owners, and dates.

![][image4]

#### **Admin view: Planner.** 

A more functional view of the client’s 90 day plan that allows for customization.

## 7\. Data model

### **7.1 Postgres (Supabase) — core tables**

* **clients** — one row per engagement; the isolation key everything scopes to.

* **client\_users** — client-side users mapped to a client\_id (RLS boundary).

* **staff** — Corduroy staff with role; **staff\_assignments** maps staff to clients.

* **plans** / **milestones** / **tasks** — structured projection of the plan JSON.

* **plan\_edits** — advisor edits captured for the edit-rate quality signal.

* **audit\_events** — append-only access/update log (who, client, object, when, why).

* **vault\_objects** — the catalog: one row per S3 object (client\_id, key, prefix, type, source, size, created\_at), maintained by the ObjectCreated event and the reconciliation job (§5.5).

* **dashboard\_widgets** (Launch 2\) — one jsonb row per widget; the serving projection the dashboard reads. Noted here so Launch 1's structured-data layer leaves room for it (§11).

### **7.2 S3 (Vault) — per client**

raw/ (source pulls and uploads) · derived/ (plan JSON, extracted artifacts) · context/ (AI-ready summaries, indices, markdown) · audit/ (append-only mirror of audit events). All written as append-only, date-stamped objects.

## 8\. Cross-cutting security architecture

* **Isolation:** one bucket \+ one KMS key per client; RLS scopes every relational query by client\_id.

* **Least privilege:** only the Retrieval Service role can touch S3, scoped per request to a single client.

* **No in-place change:** data is written append-only as dated objects and never overwritten; destructive operations are not exposed in any UI. History is preserved by convention rather than by Object Lock or versioning (see §5.1).

* **ZDR LLM path:** models receive context through the Retrieval Service over a zero-data-retention tier and retain nothing.

* **Secrets:** S3/KMS credentials exist only as the Retrieval Service's AWS execution role — never stored on Railway, in the frontends, or with any LLM.

* **Encryption:** SSE-KMS at rest, TLS in transit; VPC endpoints keep Retrieval-to-S3 traffic on the AWS network.

* **Audit & tamper-evidence:** append-only Postgres log mirrored to an append-only S3 prefix, with no delete path exposed.

## 9\. Environments & deployment

* Vercel: one project serving the Client App on app.corduroy.com and the Admin Console on admin.corduroy.com, with preview / staging / production.

* Railway: Orchestration API service with staging and production environments.

* Supabase: one project with staging and production instances; migrations version-controlled.

* AWS: one account, dedicated VPC; Retrieval Service (Lambda/App Runner), per-client buckets, KMS keys, VPC endpoints. Per-client S3 cost tagging from day one for later pricing validation.

* Secrets managed per environment; no production secret ever present in a frontend bundle.

## 10\. Open questions & decisions to confirm

| Decision | Recommendation / note |
| :---- | :---- |
| Codename | TBD |
| Auth topology | Decided: one Supabase project, staff/client by role with staff SSO \+ MFA. Revisit only if an enterprise security review demands separate pools. |
| Frontend topology | Decided: one codebase, Admin Console on its own subdomain. Keeps admin code server-side and leaves room to gate the admin surface later. |
| Retrieval Service host | Decided: AWS, two Lambdas (access broker \+ content processor). Not Railway — see §5.3. |
| Per-client bucket vs prefix | Per-client bucket at v1 (clean isolation); plan prefix/account sharding past \~1,000 clients. |
| Supabase SAML SSO \+ SOC 2 | Confirm Pro tier includes SAML SSO; review current SOC 2 report before client commitments rely on it. |
| Anthropic ZDR terms | Confirm ZDR tier scope/terms before the Data Security Commitment cites them. |

## 11\. Out of scope for Launch 1 (Launch 2 preview)

Designed later, but the Launch 1 architecture leaves room for them:

| In scope — Launch 1 | Deferred (Launch 2 / Phase 2\) |
| :---- | :---- |
| The Vault — per-client storage, ingestion, Retrieval Service, context generation, audit 90-Day Planner — generation, plan schema, advisor review queue, client view, edit-rate tracking One codebase, two surfaces (client app \+ admin console on separate subdomains), with role-based access and staff SSO \+ MFA | KPI Dashboard — automated KPI extraction, chart views, milestone linkage (Launch 2\) Daily Coaching \+ HITL — daily check-ins, escalation queue, advisor intervention (Launch 2\) QuickBooks and other connectors; desktop agentic retrieval; automated refresh (Phase 2\) |

### **Forward-looking note: the custom-KPI widget model (Launch 2\)**

Captured now because it shapes the content processor's output contract, not because it is built in Launch 1\. Most metrics (\~80%) are standard — cash flow, headcount, revenue — and come from a shared library of metric definitions. A fraction are custom and unique to a client (the LLM derives the metric and its calculation rules from that client's Audit document). The key design seam is that the metric is unique but its presentation is standard.

* **Each widget is a small self-contained JSON object with two zones: a presentation contract the LLM must conform to (type: bar / line / progress / stat, palette token, label, unit, goal) and a free-form data zone** for the metric values. The frontend only needs to render the handful of standard types; it never has to understand the novel metric.

* **Widget JSON is validated against the presentation schema in the content processor before storage,** so a malformed or off-contract object is rejected at generation time rather than surfacing as a broken tile. Standard and custom metrics converge into one validated code path; only the provenance of the metric definition differs.

* **Stored as jsonb rows in dashboard\_widgets and rendered directly from Postgres** (the serving rule, §5.5). The fuller JSON artifact still lands in derived/ for provenance and regeneration, but the dashboard reads the row, not the file.

One implication to carry: KPIs are only as fresh as the last raw-file ingest, so the Launch 2 dashboard should surface an “as of” timestamp — a stalled ingest pipeline then shows as a visible date rather than a silently stale number.

# 12\. Build sequence (To-Do)

Ordered so the standard SaaS scaffolding lands first, then the Vault (the non-negotiable prerequisite that every later feature depends on), then the Planner. Each phase ends with a checkpoint before the next begins.

## Phase 0 — Scaffolding (standard SaaS functionality)

1. Containerize and lay down the repo: Dockerfiles for the services, one Next.js codebase serving the client app and admin console on separate subdomains, a shared component library.

2. Stand up environments and CI/CD: a Vercel project (app \+ admin subdomains), a Railway service, one Supabase project, and an AWS account with a VPC — each with staging and production.

3. Authentication and app shell: Supabase Auth (one pool, role-based), staff SSO \+ mandatory MFA via email-domain routing, and role-gated routing separating the client and admin surfaces.

4. Core relational schema: clients, client\_users, staff, staff\_assignments, audit\_events, vault\_objects.

## Phase 1 — The Vault

5. Provision per-client storage as infrastructure-as-code: one S3 bucket and one KMS key per client.

6. Build AccessBroker (Lambda): validate, mint pre-signed GET/PUT URLs with server-built keys, append audit rows. Scope Railway's AWS credential to invoke-only.

7. Wire ingestion: the upload flow (AccessBroker mints the PUT URL; the browser uploads direct to S3) and the S3 ObjectCreated trigger.

8. Build ContentDispatcher (Lambda): pull per-type/per-client handling rules from the API, extract the relevant content, route it onward (usually to the API), write derived artifacts, and update the catalog. Hold its Supabase credential in Secrets Manager, scoped insert-only to the catalog and audit tables.

9. Build the catalog and the reconciliation / true-up job (reconcile, never rebuild).

10. Build the Vault views (§5.8): the add-source pane and the data repository.

11. Security checkpoint: verify no cross-client access by review, stand up the canary health check, and write the break-glass runbook (§5.7).

## Phase 2 — The 90-Day Planner

12. Build the generation pipeline: prompt architecture producing a structured plan from the Audit document \+ Vault context.

13. Define the plan schema and project it into Postgres rows.

14. Build the advisor review queue and edit-rate tracking.

15. Build the client plan view (§6.4): read, mark complete, comment.

16. Real-client test (Elevated Concrete Solutions) end-to-end against the Launch 1 definitions of done.
